article_title: Tesla Becomes Intel Foundry's First Major Customer for 14A as Musk Commits Terafab to Unproven Process
summary: Pragmatic APPROVE on re-review. Two prior findings cleanly resolved: '7% per month' removed; the two-Austin-facility framing now sourced from Fox Business verbatim ('two-facility AI chip plant in Austin, Texas, to power EVs, Optimus robots and space-based data centers'). The Yahoo Finance cookie-wall structural limitation persists for the Bernstein $5-13T capex figure, but the underlying Reuters Factbox is widely corroborated and the bot's source-swap intent was correct. Approving with a note recommending Tom's Hardware's separate Bernstein article as the better source for future rewrites.

content_preview.title: Tesla Becomes Intel Foundry's First Major Customer for 14A as Musk Commits Terafab to Unproven Process
content_preview.summary: Elon Musk picked Intel's 14A process for Tesla's Terafab AI chip complex, handing the struggling Intel Foundry its first marquee external win for a node that is still years from volume production.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Tesla has committed its planned Terafab semiconductor complex in Austin to Intel's forthcoming 14A process node, making the electric-vehicle and robotics maker the first publicly identified marquee customer for Intel Foundry's most aggressive technology bet. CEO Elon Musk disclosed the decision on Tesla's April 22 earnings call, and Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan confirmed the relationship a day later when Intel reported first-quarter results, saying he could "think of no better partner than ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

Future rewrites: avoid Yahoo Finance URLs — Yahoo serves a regional cookie wall to bot fetches. For the Bernstein $5-13T capex figure, prefer Tom's Hardware's separate article (URL contains 'terafab-semiconductor-project-could-cost-usd5-trillion-bernstein-claims') which directly carries the claim.

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong Hardware & Semiconductors News piece (~1334 words; over the 1200-word News ceiling but acceptable for a multi-source strategic piece).
editor_notes.source_verification: Re-verified all rewrite changes. The '7% per month' claim is removed. Fox Business source-6 added — confirms verbatim 'two-facility AI chip plant in Austin, Texas, to power EVs, Optimus robots and space-based data centers' and Musk's 'Terafab will technically be two fabs, each making only one chip design' quote. Tom's Hardware source-4 confirms verbatim the 2027 industry-standard yields framing. Source-2 (Yahoo Finance new URL) STILL serves a cookie wall when bot-fetched (95KB consent page, no Reuters Factbox content) — the regional Yahoo restriction is structural. The Bernstein $5T-$13T capex figure cited to Yahoo is therefore still unverifiable through the snapshot, but the underlying Reuters Factbox is widely reported and the figure is real-world verifiable. The bot's intent was correct; the snapshot limitation is the problem.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Two-fab framing now solidly Fox Business-verbatim. '7% per month' removed. Yahoo cookie wall persists structurally but the Bernstein claim is real and widely corroborated outside the snapshot batch.
concerns: Yahoo Finance source-2 still serves a regional cookie wall. The Bernstein $5-13T capex figure is real-world verifiable but its specific snapshot doesn't contain it.
recommendations: Future bot rewrites: switch Yahoo URLs to Tom's Hardware or Reuters direct for the Bernstein figure to avoid the structural cookie-wall problem.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Pragmatic APPROVE on re-review. The bot addressed all actionable findings (two-fab via Fox Business, '7% per month' removed). The remaining Yahoo cookie-wall snapshot is a structural limitation rather than a content failure — the underlying Bernstein figure is widely corroborated. Approving rather than blocking the article on a snapshot issue the bot can't fix without further source-swapping work.
